ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for packaged plum drink; 'fruit drink' (FOODON_03301737) is the closest defensible parent class for a sweetened, flavoured, ready-to-drink fruit-based beverage.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Fruit nectars and drinks) is the closest defensible category for a packaged plum-flavoured sweetened beverage. No exact FoodEx2 entry exists for plum drink specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for packaged plum drink; omitted to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving is one can or bottle of approximately 250 ml, equivalent to 250 g, commonly consumed as a cold beverage with meals or as a refreshment.

nutrient_source.title: Plum drink (packaged) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for packaged plum drink (cr_id D05010034). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for packaged plum drink in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 52 is estimated by analogy to sweetened fruit drinks with similar sugar content (~8g/100g sucrose/glucose mix) from the University of Sydney GI Database (e.g., fruit punch and similar sweetened beverages GI range 47–67, median ~52). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (8.5g/100g × 250g = 21.25g) / 100 = 11.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (180 mg/100 g) combined with high sugar (8.0 g/100 g) — sugar-driven insulin response promotes renal sodium retention, compounding direct sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage; sodium at 180 mg/100 g (450 mg per 250 ml serving) contributes significantly to daily sodium limits, and the 8.0 g/100 g sugar content independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines — choose unsweetened water, plain tea, or water infused with fresh plum slices instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 8.0 g sugar/100 g and zero dietary fibre — liquid sugar is absorbed rapidly, causing acute postprandial blood glucose spikes
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this packaged plum drink entirely; the 8.0 g/100 g free sugar with no fibre buffer and an estimated GI of 52 delivers approximately 21 g of rapidly absorbed carbohydrate per 250 ml serving, causing significant glycaemic excursions — unsweetened plum tea brewed from dried plums without added sugar is a safer alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sugar content (8.0 g/100 g) in a liquid form with elevated sodium (180 mg/100 g) and no protective nutrients (zero fibre, zero omega-3, no polyphenols at meaningful concentration)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this beverage; regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased CVD risk (AHA/ACC 2019), and the 180 mg/100 g sodium adds to daily cardiovascular sodium burden — replace with plain water, unsweetened herbal tea, or low-sodium, no-added-sugar alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar with no anti-inflammatory nutrients; sodium benzoate preservative may trigger inflammatory responses in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this packaged plum drink as it provides no anti-inflammatory benefit (no omega-3, meaningful polyphenols, fibre, or antioxidants) and the added sugar may promote pro-inflammatory mediators — choose whole fresh plums or unsweetened plum tea for any plum-derived benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Packaged plum drink is a commercially manufactured, ready-to-drink sweetened beverage popular in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. It is produced by diluting plum extract or concentrate with water, adding sugar, citric acid for tartness, and sodium benzoate as a preservative. The drink has a sweet-sour flavour profile characteristic of preserved plum (话梅, hua mei), with a light amber to reddish-brown colour and a thin, watery texture. It is commonly sold in cans or bottles of 250–330 ml and consumed chilled as a refreshment with meals or as a standalone drink. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie beverage at 32 kcal per 100 g, but it is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage with 8.0 g of free sugar per 100 g and zero dietary fibre, meaning the sugar is absorbed rapidly. Sodium content is notably high at 180 mg per 100 g, largely from preservatives, contributing approximately 450 mg sodium per 250 ml serving. Protein and fat are absent by product formulation. The drink is not suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its sugar and sodium profile. It carries no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients and is not recommended for rheumatoid arthritis management. The plum extract concentration in commercial packaged versions is typically insufficient to deliver clinically meaningful polyphenol benefit. Healthier alternatives include unsweetened plum tea brewed from dried plums or water infused with fresh plum slices.

Is packaged plum drink high in sugar?

meal_context: cold beverage with hawker centre meals

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D05010034) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The food is a canonical packaged beverage with a well-defined formulation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, which slightly reduces overall certainty for the GI component, but all other values are exact-match from an approved primary source.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero by product formulation — this is a water-based sweetened beverage containing no protein ingredients. fat_g is confirmed zero by product formulation — no fat-containing ingredients are present. saturated_fat_g and cholesterol_mg are confirmed zero consistent with zero total fat. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — the plum extract concentration is insufficient to contribute measurable fibre. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in HPB Foodo database for this beverage. potassium_mg set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo database for this entry; estimated ~20 mg/100 g based on typical plum drink formulations but not confirmed by approved source, therefore set to -1. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported in HPB Foodo database for this packaged beverage entry. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened fruit drinks of similar sugar composition from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for packaged plum drink was found in any approved GI source. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = 52 × 21.25 / 100 = 11.1 (medium GL). The dietary_tag 'low_calorie' from the input is retained as contextually accurate (32 kcal/100 g) but does not override the high concern ratings for sugar and sodium in clinical contexts.
